blocks.content: Preparations always take longer than planned. The goal is not to have a beautifully furnished van right away, but to ensure the basics function.

images.alt: The solar charge controller and the inverter
The bed
A stable internet connection for online work

blocks.content: There is a bed to sleep on, the electricity works and there is an internet connection, so we can leave for a test drive. But then comes an unexpected turn of events: the van is not yet fully mechanically sound, so we cannot leave yet! When traveling in a van, you always have to be able to handle unexpected twists. Adjusting plans, and above all realizing that this is not so bad at all, and that traveling is largely about this ability to adapt. The advantage now is that I can start doing that even before a test drive has begun!

The van was checked mechanically and we are ready to hit the road! There might be a few extra things that need to be done later, but for now, we are on our way. So, really picking up the van next week! And I promise I will post more photos then too.

It was actually an advantage to wait another week, because now some extra things could be done. The solar panels have been permanently attached, the wardrobe has been installed, and the gas stove has also passed its first test. Tomorrow a few final preparations, and then off for some test drives!

images.alt: The toilet
When the toilet is closed, it's just a normal seat
The second seat, aka the closet
Stove
Solar panels

blocks.content: The long drive to Belgium went well, and then a short trip to a beautiful lake in the area. Tomorrow we leave the solid soil behind and embark on the real journey.

The first steps to get on the way were to pack all my stuff, store it in the van and clean the apartment. This was pretty intense, but with great help it all worked out very well! (Thank you W&J!!) Then most of my stuff was offloaded to give away, and some to store, and then this picture shows what's left. Before it nearly reached the ceiling of the van! You always have more stuff than you thought, even after you scaled down already in preparation to move into the van.

images.alt: Even after getting rid of most stuff, inside it's still a mess (with also some nice things! ;)

Instead of the picture at the top of this post, I might also have shown this one. A bit less messy, I cleaned up just to take this picture. But what's the best to show on a blog, the messy reality or sometimes a slightly cleaner version? (What do you think? Let me know in the comments below! Oh no...:D

blocks.content: Then the real journey starts. The electricity suddenly didn't work the first day, but that turned out to be just a low battery. The water pump keeps running, so I just disconnect it when not needed. The teething troubles of a 'new' van. But we're on the road, first visiting the Dutch National Education Museum in Dordrecht, and then the nearby Nature reserve De Biesbosch.

images.alt: Education museum
Biesbosch 1
Biesbosch 2
Biesbosch 3
Biesbosch 4
Biesbosch 5

blocks.content: It has already been a week, so it is time for a new post. Last week I was still in the Netherlands, where the journey took me past the tulip fields and included a visit to my first student city, Leiden. Then followed the first really big trip to Switzerland, where I continued the journey alone as of yesterday. Traveling alone or with someone is a big difference; now the real challenge begins of being 'on the road', working, eating, relaxing, and reflecting while traveling.

images.alt: The place where it all started nearly 20 years ago
The flower fields next to Keukenhof
On the road in Germany. Thanks to a lovely photographer for this beautiful picture.

blocks.content: Besides work starting again, there is also time for relaxation: a walk to one of the peaks in the Jura Mountains in France, with a view over the Geneva Lake and the Mont Blanc. Fortunately, the ski season is already over, so it is quiet here. For those following this blog for the beautiful photos like the ones below: it will be a little while before I come across such spectacular views again. Starting tomorrow, I will slowly travel further north again, to pass by Belgium and the Netherlands next week.

images.alt: An early guest at the parking place this morning
The empty ski slope
The ski slope from above
The melting snow on the hiking trail
The road appears again
Summit
Finding back my footsteps on the way back
Where is the path?
Back home. For the exact route of this walk, see my Komoot account.

blocks.content: This time, a slightly more extensive update. On the way back to Leuven and the Netherlands, there are two places I wanted to visit, and the first of these is the canal pictured above. This canal is special for several reasons. It connects the Seine with the Saône and Rhône, making it possible for the first time to reach the Mediterranean Sea from Paris via France's inland waterways. The construction of the canal is impressive, as a large difference in elevation had to be overcome. This resulted in 189 locks and, at the highest point, a tunnel more than 3 km long. But for me personally, this canal is also special because I stumbled upon it by chance during my cycling trip through Europe in the summer of 2013. After conquering many mountains and hills, it was a relief to be able to follow a cycling path along the water. Almost thirteen years ago, at that time already I thought: when I am a bit older, and no longer making such a trip with a bike and a tent, I will do it in a van. And now I am here, cycling on my mountainbike along those same bike paths as almost 13 years ago. What a different journey, and what a different world. Back then, I didn't have a smartphone yet, and in the beginning, I even set off without a map. I sent a text message once a week to let my family know where I was. Now I use my smartphone constantly: to look up where I can park the van for the night, for navigation, I look up where I can refuel or do groceries, and I search for hiking and cycling trails via Komoot. Moreover, I work online and maintain this blog. How different this journey is now compared to 13 years ago, but also: how different the world is now. The contrast with 13 years ago makes me reflect on that as well. I am happy with all the online options that make this current journey possible, and at the same time, when the news comes along or when I see titles on YouTube screaming for attention, the world of 2026 feels so much more chaotic than the world of 2013. Part of this current journey is also a detachment from a frenzied existence, and at the same time, I do not want it to be a flight, a withdrawal from responsibility for this world. Where are all these considerations leading? Hopefully, that will become clearer over the next months.

images.alt: The van on the left, next to the water basin at the highest point of the canal
Further along the canal becomes narrower
To supply the canal at its highest point of enough water, this basin was constructed
At the village Pouilly-en-Auxois the canal disappears underground
Even though it is narrower, it still is an impressive waterway, that was excavated in the rock over a length of 3333 meter with handdrills and black powder (in the years 1826-1832)
Keeping a good hold of my phone while taking this picture!
To construct the tunnel, shafts were built that needed to reach up to 40 meters deep to reach the canal level
Altitude of the shaft above sea level
From 1892 onwards an electric tugboat was used to pull ships through the tunnel
The houses along the canal that were in the past inhabited by the lockkeepers are also interesting
Some have a more modern attachment built to them
Do the children really play this close to the water?
Some houses are abandoned

blocks.content:
The second destination I absolutely wanted to visit was Verdun. Already when I cycled through northern France 13 years ago, I realized that it would be much easier to visit the various historical sites connected to the First World War with a van. And where better to start than in Verdun? 
The first destination was the citadel, an underground stronghold from which the city's defense was coordinated. A modern tour through the citadel's underground tunnels consists of a ride in a little self-driving vehicle for max 5 people, where you put on VR glasses to see various scenes from that time in the corridors. However, the result remains somewhat disappointing. Typically enough, the story told during the tour is about 4 soldiers coming from the front line to attend a ceremony. The underground citadel is safe, where the soldiers' raw experiences cannot find a place. All of this comes a little closer when you arrive in Fleury-devant-Douaumont. This small village was right on the front line, and was captured by the Germans multiple times and recaptured by the French. It was completely destroyed and never rebuilt, but the streets and houses of that time have been made visible again by street signs and stones marking the houses, shops, and other buildings. All of this is situated in a landscape where the bomb craters are still visible. Even as you drive further through these woods, that is the scene: a crater landscape, reclaimed by nature, yet still clearly visible. Perhaps that speaks even more than all the memorial stones for peace and brotherhood erected here and there, the raw power of the shells, the wounds in the earth, still so clearly visible after exactly 110 years. 
And then you drive further, out of the forest, and you reach the first inhabited vilage again. Here people live, in warmth and shelter. Precious and fragile.

images.alt: Trees in bomb craters
Monument in the city of Verdun
The village
The War Cemetery with 16.000 graves
War Cemetery with view on the Ossuary
In the Ossuary the remains of 130.000 not identified soldiers are laid to rest
The entrance of the Citadel

blocks.content: Besides all these heavy themes, van life continues in its everyday aspects. Cooked a real meal for the first time, cooked rice for the first time using energy from the solar panels. And some final images from the Jura Mountains, with two beautiful lakes, and the first short mountain bike ride I took there.

images.alt: An oldfashioned meal
Cooking on solar energy
Lac des Rousses
Testing the MTB
Lac de Chalain
Remains of an old castle at Lac de Chalain
Interesting sedimented limestone layers at the roadside
When you have a big task in front of you, think about this bever
Nature reserve at this morning's parking place
The host of the nature reserve

blocks.content: Lately, I’ve been wondering: what is missing from the image I project with my blog posts? Which part of my van life am I not showing? When I mainly show the places I visit, maybe I am no better than people who just show beautiful pictures on Instagram? Looking back, I had indeed kept an important part of the past few weeks unmentioned. Particularly regarding the trip to Switzerland, there were doubts about whether this van, which I bought last autumn but had been sitting idle for a while before that, would be able to handle it. In early April, during the first trip through the Netherlands, there was also a moment when a lot of smoke suddenly came out of the exhaust. Fortunately, I had good remote assistance, the problem was diagnosed correctly, and I was able to continue driving (A first time many thanks to H.!!). In the days that followed, I did constantly check the oil level, and although it was slightly too high, the decision was made to drive to Switzerland anyway. It is a slightly older van with a not-so-powerful engine, and some parts will be checked again in the coming weeks. But the feeling that you are not entirely sure about your mode of transport/home can certainly be categorized under the title 'challenges of van life'. I am not entirely sure why I hadn't mentioned this in the blog before, but this week I have a good opportunity to give it my full attention. Because earlier this week, there was suddenly a day when the engine simply wouldn't start... (read more below the photos)

images.alt: Checking the oil level every morning
Here's where the problem was/is situated probably
Picture taken from below

blocks.content:
The battery had enough voltage, so it wasn't just a matter of connecting the jumper cables to another car. But fortunately, this was once again properly diagnosed remotely (thanks again H.!!!), and the problem likely turned out to be in the electrical system. When you press the relay (grey block above), proper contact is made and the engine does start. Now, every time I start, the question is whether the engine will actually start, and whether everything will go well until I return to the Netherlands next week. However, the interesting thing about all this is primarily the mental aspect: surrendering to the uncertainty, being able to make peace with it when all your plans are thwarted, and when unexpected costs suddenly arise. Staying calm, anticipating negative outcomes, but at the same time not letting your mood drag down because of it. It remains a challenge every time, but these incidents are important points in that process.
But besides all these practical and mental concerns, there are sometimes beautiful places you come across: a parking lot specifically for vans in a little Flemish town, with toilets, water, and a drain to empty your toilet. And that right next to a beautiful park.
Next week I am going back to the Netherlands to work on the van a bit and to have the engine checked further, so the next update will focus more on the van's interior.

images.alt: The park with the van hidden among the leaves
There are also some animals in the park...
...with most beautifully this peacock

blocks.content:
The plan was to post an update every week, but that hasn't really happened lately. The trip was also on hold for a while due to updates to the camper, and my attention was focused more on family in the last week. I am back on the road now, currently in the Ardennes at a beautiful spot next to a farm.
I hope to post another update in about a week; if you would like to receive an email notification for a new update, you can let me know under the tab Contact above.

images.alt: Farm in the Ardennes
Goats
Ardennes field
This shelf above the kitchen areas comes in handy to store stuff
The ceiling has gotten wooden slats and spotlights

blocks.content: Another longer update this week. The lake pictured above looks like an ordinary, tranquil spot in nature, but it turns out that it has a fascinating story behind it. And not just here; throughout the Eifel region, there are various special lakes that have a unique tale to tell. These 'Eyes of the Eifel' were formed by volcanoes erupting upon contact with groundwater, a process that blasted away layers of rock, creating a deep, funnel-shaped cavity. Once the volcanic activity subsided, the funnel filled with water, giving rise to lakes encircled by a rim formed from the material ejected from the crater during the eruption. Such a volcanic lake is also known as a 'maar' (derived from the Latin word 'mare', meaning 'sea'). Some lakes, such as the one shown above (Lake Immerath), are older and relatively shallow. This particular lake was even drained for agricultural use for a time, only to be submerged once again later on. The youngest of these lakes is the Ulmener lake, which remains tens of meters deep. Moreover, it possesses a unique feature: on the outer slope of its rim, one can clearly observe the distinct layers left behind by the various eruptions. The volcanic origin of these formations is evident not only from the solidified basalt fragments scattered amidst the rubble, but also from the fact that larger stones and fine debris were hurled skyward together before falling back down here. There is even a single boulder among them measuring nearly a meter across.

images.alt: Immerather maar from the top of the rim
Another deeper maar, the Holzmaar
Pulvermaar

A maar that has since completely filled in. This volcano was unique because the eruption emerged laterally from a hill, and consequently did not form a circular rim, but rather the hill visible in the background of this photograph.
The Meerfelder maar
The Meerfeld Maar, too, is half-dry; you can see that the small village of Meerfeld, situated on the opposite side of the lake, lies within the rim of the original maar.
Jungferweiher, a shallow maar right next to the Ulmener maar
The Ulmener maar
The back of the rim surrounding the Ulmener Maar. You can distinguish the various layers from the different eruptions.
The meter-sized boulder amidst the crushed layers.
An information board explaining the different rock layers.
The information board

blocks.content: There is yet another reason why the Ulmener Maar is so remarkable. Layers can be found not only within the rim surrounding the volcano but also on the bottom of the maar itself. Once the water level in the maar had stabilized, annual deposits consisting of algae and other microorganisms, among other things, began to accumulate. Because there is no oxygen at the bottom of such a deep maar, no life can exist there; consequently, these layers are sometimes beautifully preserved. The ELSA project conducted boreholes in 20 different maars, reaching depths of up to 100 meters, and subsequently analyzed the resulting sediment cores. The deposits were found to be particularly well-preserved in the Holzmaar and the Ulmener Maar, making it possible to distinguish between summer (characterized by a lighter layer consisting primarily of plankton) and winter (a darker layer of organic material) dating back thousands of years. These layers are known as 'varves'. Not only can one count the years by examining the alternating light and dark layers, but the thickness of these layers also provides clues regarding the living conditions and climate of that era. The Ulmener Maar is the youngest maar in the Eifel region (10,900 years old); the Holzmaar is somewhat older (23,000 years old); however, the oldest of all is the Eckfelder Maar (43 million years old)—a site situated deep within the forest that is no longer recognizable as a maar, yet where numerous fossils have been discovered and boreholes have also been drilled.

images.alt: In the Maar Museum in the small village of Manderscheid, there stands a replica of a drill core, along with several small samples of actual drill cores.
The information board at the Holzmaar
The specific spot within the original Eckfeld site where excavations are being conducted.
Information board at the Eckfelder maar

blocks.content: Finally, included below are some miscellaneous photos—mostly from various bike rides—along with a lovely story. What would've happened to me had I lived 500 years ago? That was the question that crossed my mind when, during a bike ride, I came across the following story hanging inside a small hut.

images.alt: The hut named 'Kobesloch'
The story in German
With help of AI the story translated into English.
The path down
Around the corner of this narrow path is the cave
A lookout tower I passed during a mountain bike ride.
The view from within the lookout tower

All the photos above were taken in the region known as the 'Vulkaneifel'. To the north lies the Eifel National Park where dams for fresh water provision and electricity generation result in several beautiful lakes surrounded by forests.
Een dam
wooden bridge
Also some hikes in the forrest; crossing a river on this steel bridge
View on an old castle from between the trees
The castle from close up

Finally, here is another lovely story. Along a bicycle path, a small sign suddenly appeared bearing the name 'Teufelsstein' (Devil's Stone). When I followed the path leading uphill, I found this. The folklore behind it turns out to be that a man building a church outwitted the Devil, tricking him into helping gather stones for the building. When the Devil discovered that the structure in question was a church, he dropped the stone right here.

title: And back home again for a little while

blocks.content: This time just a quick update, now that I'm back in Belgium and the Netherlands for a little while. Next week I'll be working on the van again, which means traveling is on hold for a bit. The photos below still show the beautiful side of van life, but at the same time, there are the difficult sides too. Mechanically, it's running fine at the moment (fingers crossed), but there are plenty of little things that can always throw you off balance. Ants in the van, falling off the mountain bike once, or just the fluctuating feeling of good and bad days. You have days when everything doesn't go so well, when you 'walk around carrying your soul under your arm' as we say in Dutch. The idea that such a feeling is solved by living in a van is, of course, incorrect. Let's just say that I am getting more and more used to living in a van, and integrating difficult experiences, big and small, is part of that.

images.alt: A last view over the Eifel
What could this thing be?
The inside. A modern version of a chapel?
The castle ruins in the old town of Kronenburg
The old town of Kronenburg apparently had such a 'Germanic' aura that, during the time of National Socialism, Hermann Göring founded a painting school here to train 'Aryan' artists of that era.
Next to the village of Kronenburg lies a lake where you can also swim, which came in very handy during the hottest days of the past week.
Back in Leuven, nostalgically cycling through Meerdaalwoud
After that in the Netherlands, here the harbour of the beautiful town Veere. So lucky to have family living at the 'Zeeuwse kust':)
The old city defense
The old cityhall
View on the old buildings from a park
The old church of Veere
In the park surrounding the church, there are descriptions of people who used to be in Veere. Among them was the writer Nescio.

title: Working on the van - sick - and on the road again

blocks.content: It has already been two weeks since the last update, and the title of this post reflects the most important events of that timeframe. As I mentioned in the last post, I was mainly at home working on the van (and getting it certified again). I am now back on the road, with today a bike ride to the Belgian-Dutch village of Baarle Hertog - Baarle Nassau.

images.alt: The most important visible upgrade to the van is the wood on the left wall
From Tornhout in Belgium, 8 km from the Dutch border, there is a cycle path to Baarle Hertog - Baarle Nassau, through a beautiful fen area
View over the fen area
The cycle path (seen here from the tower) lies on the route of an old Belgian railway line to Tilburg, popularly known as the 'Bels lijntje'. That is now the name of the cycling path
The old border station next to the bike path
5 km further into the Netherlands, in Baarle, there is suddenly the Belgian border again
In addition to the trip to Baarle, another visit to Leuven, here the view of a new park in the city center
Visiting a few old friends in Leuven

blocks.content:
Visiting and documenting sights, such as the one from Baarle above, feels more and more like a distraction from what this journey is really about. Under the heading 'The Journey' on this website, I wrote three months ago: 'What this journey is exactly, will probably only become clear over time.' There is certainly no clear answer to that question yet, but I do sense various tendencies. Visiting sights is to someone who travels what children are to someone who has settled down: it gives meaning, purpose, and direction to your existence. But there remains something unsatisfying about having to visit new things every time, as Ecclesiastes already said: 'The eye is not satisfied with seeing.' And at the same time, it has an appeal; you know where you want to go, and it is fun to share with others. I will continue to do that, too, but I do not want to lose sight of the other things that feel more essential: reflecting, reading, and writing. This can be done by pausing a bit more along the way, but even a break like the past week provides food for reflection. Being sick for a few days (which, by the way, meant not as much work was done on the van as I would have liked) also makes you reflect on the contrast between the traveling existence in which I prefer to fend for myself, and the stable base of home, where it is nice to have family nearby, especially when you are sick. Moreover, being sick is in itself a ground for reflection, feeling how existence is at the mercy of processes that take their own course; the fever that comes and goes, which you try to attune to with rest, food, and sometimes some medication, but where you are always just waiting to see how your body reacts, and where some surrender is required. Even with something as minor as a bad cold or a mild flu, you already feel something of all the existential themes hidden within it.
So, I will take things a bit easier this coming week, but there are plenty of other things going on, such as finishing my training. In just over a week, I hope to return to the Netherlands to carry out (or have carried out) the final work (for now) on the camper (both interior and mechanical), so that I can leave for a slightly longer period in July. (destination: France)
